summary: Gruber criticizes Warner Bros. Discovery's decision to rebrand HBO Max as simply 'Max,' arguing it destroys one of the most prestigious brands in entertainment. He compares the streaming service's pricing tiers to competitors like Netflix, Disney+, and Apple TV+, finding them roughly equivalent. He contends that cramming HBO's premium content alongside Discovery's reality TV programming into a single app dilutes the HBO brand beyond recognition, and that Disney's approach of keeping Disney+ and Hulu as separate bundled apps was the smarter model. The piece mourns the loss of HBO's identity as a marker of premium, ad-free content.
key_points: By combining all of Warner Bros. Discovery's disparate brands into one service, none of them stand out and the 'Max' brand means nothing
Disney set the better example by keeping Disney+ and Hulu as separate apps with bundle pricing, preserving each brand's identity
HBO is ceding its 'everything here is premium, no ads' positioning to Apple TV+
The 'Max' name unfortunately evokes Cinemax — HBO's less prestigious sibling — rather than building on HBO's iconic brand equity
The pricing tiers are competitive with Netflix and Disney+ but the brand strategy undermines what made HBO special
money_quotes: I would argue the opposite: that by cramming their entire disparate gamut of brands into a single service, none of them stand out, and the Max brand stands for nothing.

HBO's old slogan was 'It's not TV. It's HBO'. That slogan captured HBO's prestige: unedited major motion pictures, best-of-breed original content, all without commercial interruptions. HBO's new slogan might as well be 'It's not TV. It's a tab somewhere in the Max app.'
They're ceding the 'everything you get on this service is premium content, and there are no ads' ground to Apple TV+.
The non-HBO content in HBO Max already diluted the brand; now they're putting even more non-premium content (all the Discovery crap) into the same app.
Disney isn't relegating Disney+ content into a mere tab in the Hulu app, nor are they allowing Hulu's content to dilute the prestigious Disney brand in the Disney+ app.
No matter what Warner Bros. Discovery intends 'Max' to mean, it feels damn stupid to lean even vaguely on Cinemax, and not at all on HBO, as a brand name.
HBO is Joe DiMaggio; Cinemax is Dom DiMaggio.
key_insight: Rebranding HBO Max to just 'Max' sacrifices one of entertainment's most iconic premium brands on the altar of corporate synergy, turning HBO from a prestige standard-bearer into a mere tab in an app full of reality TV.
